payload.summary: Verified the amendment against the actual code. The cascading failure scenario is real and well-articulated. At line 5133, `run_snapshot_write.write_run_snapshot(...)` uses `?`, so if the snapshot write fails, the function returns early, skipping the run_failed journal event at lines 5136-5140. The irony is stark: the comments at lines 4643-4656 explicitly document the danger of journal/snapshot divergence and implement careful recovery via fail_run_result — but fail_run itself has the same vulnerability class. The journal event emission at 5138-5139 already uses best-effort semantics (`if let Ok` + `let _ =`), establishing that the developers intended it to be non-fatal. Applying the same best-effort pattern to the snapshot write at 5133 would be a low-risk, high-value improvement that ensures the journal event is at least attempted even under persistent disk I/O failure.

votes.rationale: Confirmed by reading the code. Line 5133 uses `?` on the snapshot write, causing early return on failure and skipping the run_failed journal event (lines 5136-5140). This is a genuine consistency defect in the error recovery path — the very path designed to prevent journal/snapshot divergence (as documented in the comments at lines 4643-4656) itself has the same class of bug. The fix is straightforward: replace the `?` at line 5133 with best-effort error handling (log the failure, continue to emit the journal event), matching the pattern already used at lines 5138-5139 for the journal append. Under persistent disk I/O failure, both writes will fail, but attempting the journal event is strictly better than bailing out. At minimum, a comment documenting this known double-failure limitation would prevent future developers from assuming the recovery path is fully robust.
